coredata.dc:title: Methanol-reduced Pd nanoparticles anchored on B,N-CDs@CNT hybrid for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yumei
Niu, Jiahong
Pei, Yanfei
Song, Haoqiang
Fan, Yanping
Lu, Siyu
Liu, Baozhong
coredata.dc:description: The development of highly active oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts with low-loading of precious metals is imperative but remains a great challenge. Herein, Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT composite catalysts was designed with Pd nanoparticles immobilized on hybrid support composed of B,N-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) and the multi-wall carbon nanotubes (CNT) using a simple methanol reduction method. The deposited Pd nanoparticles exhibit clean surface, low crystallinity and surface distortion. The Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst shows significantly enhanced ORR activity, with the mass activity about 5–6 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C. Thus prominent ORR performance is mainly attributed to the unique microstructure of Pd nanoparticles. Moreover, the composited B,N-CDs and the doped B, N heteroatoms further improve the ORR performance, in which B,N-CDs supply more absorption sites, the formation of N–Pd facilitates the electron transfer, B doping can promote the adsorption of oxygenated species and weaken the strong interaction between Pd and C. Furthermore, the B, N co-doping plays a synergistic effect. This strategy provides a simple and mild method for designing highly efficient electrocatalysts with ultra-low precious metals in alkaline electrolytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pd nanoparticles
Oxygen reduction reaction
Electrocatalysis
Carbon dots
Carbon nanotube

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure characterizations of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT: (a) TEM images (inset: particle size distribution of Pd nanoparticles in Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT); (b) HRTEM image; (c) High angle annular dark-field TEM image and corresponding EDX mapping of C, B, N, O and Pd elements.
Structural characterization of the prepared Pd-based catalysts: (a) XRD patterns; (b) Raman spectra.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1s; (b) Pd 3d; (c) N 1s; and (d) B 1s in Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT

(a) ORR measurement for the prepared Pd-based catalysts and Pt/C (20 wt%) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1; (b) the corresponding mass activities, inset of (b): the comparison of mass activity under different potentials; (c) Tafel plots calculated based on the ORR polarization curves of (a); (d) the RRDE measurement for Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT.

(a) Chronoamperometric measurement for long-term stability of catalysts at 0.7 V (vs RHE), inset of (a): Chronoamperometric curves before and after adding 1 M methanol at 0.7 V (vs RHE); (b) ORR polarization curves of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT before and after 5000 potential cycles, inset of (b): the corresponding cyclic voltammograms between 0.6 V–1.0 V Vs RHE in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 50 mV s−1; (c) TEM images, (d) HRTEM image and (e) High angle annular dark-field TEM image and corresponding EDX mapping of C, B, N, O and Pd elements of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT after 5000 potential cycles.
Comparison of the ORR performance of various Pd-based catalysts in 0.1 M KOH.

head.ce:title: Methanol-reduced Pd nanoparticles anchored on B,N-CDs@CNT hybrid for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Century Avenue, Jiaozuo City, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Century Avenue, Jiaozuo City, 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, No. 100 Science Avenue, Zhengzhou City, 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, No. 100 Science Avenue, Zhengzhou City, 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of highly active oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts with low-loading of precious metals is imperative but remains a great challenge. Herein, Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT composite catalysts was designed with Pd nanoparticles immobilized on hybrid support composed of B,N-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) and the multi-wall carbon nanotubes (CNT) using a simple methanol reduction method. The deposited Pd nanoparticles exhibit clean surface, low crystallinity and surface distortion. The Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst shows significantly enhanced ORR activity, with the mass activity about 5–6 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C. Thus prominent ORR performance is mainly attributed to the unique microstructure of Pd nanoparticles. Moreover, the composited B,N-CDs and the doped B, N heteroatoms further improve the ORR performance, in which B,N-CDs supply more absorption sites, the formation of N–Pd facilitates the electron transfer, B doping can promote the adsorption of oxygenated species and weaken the strong interaction between Pd and C. Furthermore, the B, N co-doping plays a synergistic effect. This strategy provides a simple and mild method for designing highly efficient electrocatalysts with ultra-low precious metals in alkaline electrolytes.

ce:section.ce:para: The low-temperature fuel cell, such as direct methanol/ethanol fuel cell, proton-exchange membrane fuel cells/alkaline membrane fuel cell have been considered as an ideal energy conversion system [1–4]. Hydrogen (H2) fuel cells have attracted numerous attention because of high energy density, high electrochemical conversion efficiency and absolute cleanliness of H2 fuel [5,6]. However, the cathode oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) shows slow kinetics, even on precious Pt-based catalysts. The high-loaded Pt catalysts are used to improve the power density to satisfy the practical application, but Pt has low reserves, expensive cost, defect of aggregation in the electrochemical environment after long cycles [7–9]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop non-Pt ORR catalysts with efficient performance, low cost and high stability. Pd can be an alternative to Pt for ORR because Pd possesses a similar structural characteristic to Pt, is relatively abundant on earth and exhibits promising ORR catalytic activity, especially in alkaline solution [10–12]. Now, Pd-based catalysts should be optimized to improve their ORR catalytic activity for commercial application. Normally, the approaches include the optimization of active Pd microstructure and the supports [13–15]. Such as, the introduction of hetero-metals into Pd crystalline lattice can adjust the d-band center of Pd through “geometric” effects and “ligand” effects, thus optimizing adsorption energies of the reaction species or intermediates [16,17]. The size and morphology of Pd-based nanostructured catalysts can be controlled to expose more active sites [18,19]. Furthermore, the synergistic effect between the active Pd and support can modify the electron transfer and the interactions bond, thus enhancing the ORR performance [20].

Carbon with a high specific surface area has been widely used as a support for Pd nanoparticles, also ascribing to the easy regulation of its microstructure by surface functionalizing and doping [21–23]. For the carbon-supported Pd-based catalysts, there is a strong interaction between the Pd nanoparticles and carbon, which was verified through studying the binding energies of Pd and graphene. This interaction resulted in decrease of the exposed Pd active sites and significant change in the carrier microstructure, further reducing the electrocatalytic activity of Pd [24]. Previous reports revealed that the modification of the carbon supports prominently affects the electrochemical activity of the supported Pd, where the complex interaction between Pd nanoparticles and different carbon support may change the crystal structure and morphology of Pd nanoparticles, promote the charge transfer, provide more active sites and further adjust the adsorption/desorption energies of reactive species [22,23]. Notably, heteroatoms (such as B, P, S and N) doped carbon can regulate the electronic structure of carbon to stabilize its original morphology in the electrochemical process. And heteroatom doping may facilitate the dispersion and anchoring of deposited Pd nanoparticles. Meantime, the synergistic effect between Pd and the support can further enhance electrocatalytic activity of the catalysts [24–28]. For instance, B doping can inhibit the structural evolution of carbon substrate and weaken the interaction between Pd and carbon, thus increasing the electrochemical stability and releasing more Pd active sites. And, the theoretical simulation proved that B doping promotes electron transfer between Pd and graphene carriers, facilitating the four-electron pathway of oxygen reduction [24,25]. N–Pd interaction in N-doped carbon nanotube as a support suggested that there is a charge transfer between pyridine N and Pd metal center, while the other graphitic N species covalently bonded with Pd without any charge redistribution. So, the electron property of Pd would be modified through the interaction with the support, further affecting the electrocatalytic activities [27–29]. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive researches, because that CDs possess low cost, superior photoelectric performance, and modifiable flexibility [30,31]. The abundant functional groups on the CDs surface can bind with the d orbitals of the metal to modulate metal electronic state [32–34]. The hybrid atom doped CDs are expected to be composited with the other carbon carriers to promote the adsorption and reduction of oxygen intermediates, also act as heteroatomic dopants to conduct heteroatom doping into the carbon carriers through high-temperature pyrolysis [35,36].

In this paper, B,N-CDs@CNT hybrid composited of B,N-doped CDs (B,N-CDs) and the multi-wall carbon nanotubes (CNT) was used as support. Meantime, the B,N-CDs act as doping sources to modify the CNT characterization. Pd nanoparticles were deposited on B,N-CDs@CNT hybrid (Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT) by methanol reduction method as ORR electrocatalyst. The methanol reduced Pd nanoparticles exhibit prominent specific ORR activity for these composite catalysts. The Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst shows the optimal ORR activity and durability, exhibiting higher mass activity than that of Pt/C and the other prepared samples. B and N co-doping further accelerate the ORR kinetics, in which the formation of N–Pd facilitates the electron transfer, B doping can promote the adsorption of oxygen/oxygen intermediates and weaken the strong interaction between Pd and C. The B, N co-doping plays a synergistic effect, being conducive to prompt the ORR activity.

All reagents were used of analytical purity grade. The CNT (95%) were provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd. The synthesis method of B,N-CDs@CNT was previously reported by our group [37]. Typically, the aqueous solution (180 mL) of l-aspartic acid (C4H7NO4, 24 mmol) and orthoboric acid (H3BO3, 24 mmol) were hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 8 h to obtain B,N-CDs. B,N-CDs (400 mg) and CNT (150 mg) were rinsed in deionized water, and the mixture was hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h. Then, the precipitate after centrifugation, washing and drying was pyrolysed at 800 °C for 4 h (Ar atmosphere) to prepare the B,N-CDs@CNT compound. The N-doped CNT (N-CNT), and B-doped CNT (B-CNT) carriers were prepared by replacing the B,N-CDs with l-aspartic acid or orthoboric acid.

B,N-CDs@CNT (95 mg) was dispersed in methanol (45 mL) by ultrasonic for 10 min and followed by continuously being stirred for 20 min to form homogeneous colloid solution. Palladium acetate (Pd(OAc)2, 10.638 mg) was dissolved in methanol (15 mL), and the Pd(OAc)2 solution in methanol was added dropwise into the B,N-CDs@CNT colloidal solution under stirring at 25 °C. After the reaction solution was continuously stirred for 1 h, the product was centrifuged, washed 3–4 times with methanol and then dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h to obtain Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT (the theoretical Pd content is 5 wt%). The CNT, N-CNT and B-CNT were used as supports for comparison.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on a X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku TTR3, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) with the scanning rate of 10 ° min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo Fischer, ESCALAB 250Xi using Al as the excitation source. The binding energy of the C 1s peak was set as 284.8 eV. Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TMPT was used to record the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images. Raman spectra were obtained on LabRam ARAMIS Raman detection system using a 532 nm laser. The Pd loading was confirmed on Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110).

The electrochemical properties were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D) using a conventional three-electrode system with a platinum sheet as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 3 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 450 μL ethanol + 50 μL Nafion solutions (5 wt%) by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then, the catalyst ink (15 μL) obtained was dropped onto a glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (RDE, PINE E7R9) with 5 mm diameter, yielding a catalyst loading of ∼0.46 mg cm−2. The electrolyte was an 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution containing saturated O2 used for the ORR electrochemical test. Commercial Pt/C catalysts (20 wt%, Alfa Aesar, China) were used for comparison.

Before the ORR measurements, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted in the range of 0 V–1.2 V vs RHE at the sweep rate of 500 mV s−1, 100 mV s−1 and 50 mV s−1 sequentially to activate the catalyst. A scan rate of 5 mV s−1 was used to conduct the measurement of ORR polarization curves. All potentials reported vs RHE were according to ERHE = ESCE + Eθ SCE+ 0.059 pH. Koutecky-Levich equation (Ik = IL x I/(IL- I) was used to calculate the kinetic current Ik, in which IL and I are limiting current and the measured current, respectively. The ORR mass activities with respect to Pd were calculated by normalizing Ik of the Pd total mass on the electrode. The Tafel equation (η = a+b∗log|j|) was applied to calculate the Tafel slopes from the treatment of the ORR polarization curves, where η, a, b and j refer to the overpotential, the intercept, the Tafel slope and the current density (j was calculated by normalizing I of the geometric area of GC electrode). A chronoamperometry measurement was performed at a constant potential of 0.7 V for 10 h in an alkaline solution. According to the rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) curves, the number of transferred electrons (n) and yield of % H2O2 during the ORR catalysis can be calculated using the following equations (Id, Ir and N are the measured disk electrode current, the ring electrode current and N is the collection efficiency (37%), respectively):
n =
4
I d
I d
+
I r
/ N
%
H 2
O 2
=
200
I r
/ N
I d
+
I r
/ N

B,N-CDs were introduced into CNT through hydrothermal treatment and high-temperature pyrolysis to form B,N-CDs/CNT compounds, where B,N-CDs anchored on CNT surface, exposing more active site, acting as B,N doping sources and regulating the electron distribution [37]. Pd nanoparticles were deposited on B,N-CDs/CNT supports with methanol as a reduction agent, in which any organic protective agent was avoided to obtain the clean Pd surface. The Pd loading content determined by ICP-OES is 3.2 wt%.

TEM image and particle size distribution of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT (Fig. 1 a) show that Pd nanoparticles are evenly dispersed on B,N-CDs@CNT support with a wide particle size distribution. Fig. 1b gives the corresponding HRTEM image, in which the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm and 0.2 nm measured are corresponding to the Pd (111) and (200) crystal planes, respectively, and the Pd nanoparticles exhibit poor crystallinity and surface distortion. The EDS mapping images of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT indicate the presence of Pd, C, B, N and O (Fig. 1c), where B and N atoms are uniformly doped in MWCNTs. So, except for the B,N-CDs composited with the CNTs, the B,N-CDs act as doping sources of B and N into CNTs, in which doped N bonded with C atoms may change the electron distribution [29] and B doping facilitates the adsorption of oxygen species [24,37].

Fig. 2 a gives the XRD patterns of the prepared Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT, Pd/B-CNT, Pd/N-CNT and Pd/CNT catalysts. The diffraction peaks at 26.0°, 42.8° and 43.3° are corresponding to the (002), (100) and (101) crystal facets of graphene. And The diffraction peaks at 40.2°, 46.8° and 68.3° are indexed to the (111), (200) and (220) crystal facets of Pd. The Pd XRD peaks show weak intensity with large half-height width, which may be due to the low palladium loading, poor crystallinity and small particle size. Laser Raman spectroscopy was used to further characterize the interaction between Pd and the supports (Fig. 1b). Two peaks for all samples in the Raman spectra around 1339 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 were assigned to D and G bands, respectively. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) represents a measure of the defects and crystallinity of carbon materials, where the addition of dopants can reduce the intrinsic defects. The ID/IG values of the prepared catalysts were ranked Pd/CNT > Pd/B-CNT > Pd/N-CNT > Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT. The order of ID/IG ratios of these Pd-based catalysts are the same as that of the supports without Pd loading [37], but the values of ID/IG ratio after Pd deposition all increase. The deviations of ID/IG values between the Pd-based catalysts and the pure supports are 0.193, 0.083, 0.054 and 0.134 for Pd/CNT, Pd/B-CNT, Pd/N-CNT and Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT, respectively. The increased ID/IG values are because of a strong interaction between Pd atoms and graphene carbon, causing an effect similar to heteroatomic doping into carbon substrate [24]. The difference of deviation values manifests the stronger interaction of Pd and CNT carrier, while the heteroatom doping can weaken this effect. This appropriate weakening effect between Pd and B,N doped CNT originates from the reduction of defects with filling of dopants in CNT, which maybe release more Pd active sites.

The XPS measurement was performed to represent the surface element composition and chemical states of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT (Fig. 3 ). The main elements of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT are carbon (C, 97.3%), nitrogen (N, 0.97%), palladium (Pd, 0.72%) and boron (B, 0.57%), also exhibiting achievement of the low Pd loading. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3a) can be identified as CO, C–O, C–N, C–C and C–B, locating at 287.2 eV, 286.5 eV, 285.7 eV, 284.8 eV, and 283.9 eV, respectively. The Pd 3d XPS spectrum shows that Pd is mainly in a metallic Pd state co-existed with Pd2+ (Fig. 3b). The Pd 3d XPS peaks are fitted at ∼341.1 eV (Pd0 3d3/2), ∼335.8 eV (Pd0 3d5/2), ∼342.7 eV (Pd2+ 3d3/2) and ∼337.6 eV (Pd2+ 3d5/2), respectively. In comparison with the standard Pd 3d XPS peaks of 340.4 eV (Pd0 3d3/2), the Pd XPS peaks of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT shift towards the high binding energy with 0.6–0.7 eV [38–40]. The positive shift of the Pd 3d XPS binding energies means a charge transfer typical of decreasing in the electron cloud density of Pd, clarifying the electronic state of the Pd atoms was significantly modified through the interaction with the support. Thus “electronic effect” results in properly weakening the adsorption of the oxygen/oxygen intermediates and further facilitating desorption of the oxygenated adsorbates on Pd active sites. The N 1s XPS spectrum is divided into the typical oxide N, graphite N, pyrrole N and pyridine N, locating at 403.0 eV, 401.1 eV, 399.6 eV and 398.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c). The carbon atoms adjacent to graphite N and pyridine N are often considered as active sites, the pyridine N interacts with Pd to make a charge transfer and the lone electron pairs of pyrrole N in carbon carrier can combine with Pd atoms to anchor them on carbon carrier [29,41–43]. In this case, the doped N activate the ORR kinetic process and stabilize the Pd from the oxidation corrosion. The high-resolved B 1s XPS spectrum can be fitted at 192.7 eV (B–O), 192.3 eV (BCO2), 191.8 eV (BC2O) and 191 eV (BC3), respectively (Fig. 3d). B atoms in B,N-CDs@CNT can be properly combined with oxygen species such as OH or H2O molecules to enhance their adsorption on surface of the catalyst [44,45]. And B atoms replace sp3 C atoms to translate into BC3 structure, prompting the redistribution of charge and formation of polarization site, which can act as the catalytic active sites. Furthermore, the presence of B atoms will weaken the strong interaction between Pd and carbon, facilitating to release the Pd active sites [24].

Chronoamperometric-current measurement was carried out to measure the stability of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst at a potential constant of 0.7 V through a continuous test for 20 h. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT displays favorable stability with the current retention of 74%, while the current of Pt/C decreased to 58% of the initial current. The combination properties between Pd and B,N-CDs@CNT support can well explain the excellent stability of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT. Furthermore, the Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst was tested in 1 M methanol alkaline solution through the chronoamperometric technique to evaluate the resistance of methanol poisoning in fuel cell application (inset of Fig. 5a). The current catalyzed by Pt/C catalyst decreases rapidly, while the current catalyzed by Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst barely changed after adding methanol in KOH electrolyte, declaring excellent resistance of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT towards the methanol poisoning. Meanwhile, The ORR polarization curves of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT after 5000 potential cycles with the potential range of 0.6–1.0 V (vs RHE) in 0.1 M KOH solution (Fig. 5b) were tested, in which the Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT showed high electrochemical stability with a negligible shift of E1/2 in ORR polarization curves. And we have characterized the microstructure of Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT by TEM and EDX mapping after the cyclic stability test (Fig. 5c–e). The Pd nanoparticles and the composited B,N-CDs@CNT support remain stable microstructure, in which Pd nanoparticles exhibit high dispersion with few agglomerations (Fig. 5c and d) and B, N heteroatoms uniformly distributed in B,N-CDs@CNT compound (Fig. 5e). Such, applying heteroatom doped CDs as compounded additive, there is a multiple enhancement effect with CDs composited with the heteroatoms doped into pristine supports.

In summary, Pd nanoparticles are deposited on the B,N-CDs@CNT hybrid support through the simple and mild method of methanol reduction (Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT) as ORR catalysts. The methanol reduction method avoids the use of the organic protective agent, further forming clean Pd surface. The B,N-CDs were not only composited with CNT, but also act as doping sources of B, N into CNT. The prepared Pd-based catalysts show uniform dispersion of Pd nanoparticles on supports with low crystallinity and surface distortion, which all exhibit higher ORR activities than commercial Pt/C. Among these Pd-based catalysts, the Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst exhibits the highest ORR activity, with the mass activity prominently higher than Pt/C. The properties of Pd nanoparticles mainly contribute to the optimal ORR activity of the Pd/B,N-CDs@CNT catalyst. Notably, the composited CDs and B,N doping provide more adsorption sites, promote the electron transfer, change the bond of Pd and supports and anchor the Pd atoms, resulting in improving activity and stability of Pd active sites. This method provides a feasible strategy for the synthesis of an efficient precious metal catalyst.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52071135, 51871090, U1804135, 21905253 and 51973200), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (NSFRF220201 and NSFRF200402).
